exemplar.title: To what extent is there a time complexity and output storage space difference between HEVC and AV1 codecs in terms of encoding?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is there a time complexity and output storage space difference between HEVC and AV1 codecs in terms of encoding?

strengths: Clear and effective communication of topic with explicit motivation linked to practical relevance
Well-defined, focused research question prominently stated and consistently connected to analysis
Comprehensive methodology with appropriate variables, detailed procedures, and evidence of informed selection
weaknesses: Minor phrasing lapses (e.g., awkward sentences) that slightly obscure clarity
No justification provided for sample selection criteria
Limited discussion of measurement error and its mitigation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work meets the 5â6 band descriptors across all three strands: topic and purpose are accurately and effectively communicated, the research question is focused and integrated throughout, and the methodology is complete with a clear rationale. Minor lapses in phrasing and lack of sampleâselection justification prevent a perfect score, so a 5/6 best reflects performance.

strengths: Good demonstration of subject knowledge with mostly relevant and accurate use of sources
Frequent and generally accurate application of specialist terminology
Clear explanations of key concepts supported by figures
weaknesses: Explanations occasionally surface-level and contain minor inaccuracies or typos (e.g., âdiscreateâ)
Some theoretical discussions lack depth (e.g., formal justification of Big-O notation)
Missed opportunities to deepen conceptual links (e.g., encoding parameters to complexity)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance aligns with the upper half of the 3â4 band: knowledge is good, terminology use is mostly accurate, and sources are relevant. Occasional errors and limited depth in some explanations justify a 4/6 rather than full marks.

strengths: Consistent relevance of research activities and background theory to the research question
Meaningful data analysis with comparative modeling (linear vs. quadratic) and supported numerical conclusions
Clear integration of graphs and quantitative evidence to support analytical claims
weaknesses: Discussion and evaluation are present but somewhat cursory, lacking deep statistical robustness (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals)
Limited exploration of alternative methodologies and potential biases
Suggestions for deeper critical evaluation (e.g., perceptual metrics) remain unaddressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall solidly in the top of the 4â6 band: research application and analysis are strong and well supported, while discussion shows good critical evaluation. Minor gaps in statistical rigor and depth of evaluation prevent a higher band, so 6/12 accurately reflects performance.

strengths: Logical academic structure with clear headings, contents page, and consistent formatting
Fully applied layout considerations (font consistency, captions, page numbers) that support readability
Proper use of LaTeX for equations and consistent bibliography styling
weaknesses: Some minor presentation issues (e.g., cut-off sentence, missing dot leaders in contents)
Code blocks could be better positioned under standardized headers for clarity
Occasional undefined variables in equations hinder immediate comprehension
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work largely meets the 3â4 band descriptors for structure and layout, with a coherent format and professional appearance. However, small flaws in navigation aids and completeness of text lead to a 3/4 rather than full marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: A full experimental plan is given: independent, dependent and control variables are identified; a detailed Python/HandBrake procedure is provided; sample size (20 videos) and hardware controls are explained; two data-collection scripts and resulting CSV tables are appended.  The methodology therefore covers an appropriate range of methods and shows clear evidence of informed selection (HandBrake CLI, timing, file-size extraction).  Some weaknessesâe.g. no justification of sample selection criteria and limited consideration of measurement errorâprevent a perfect score but still satisfy the upper band.

Data are processed into meaningful forms (frame counts, MiB, scatter plots).  Linear and quadratic models are compared using RÂ², yielding supported conclusions about time complexity.  The student links quantitative trends to codec features and provides concrete numerical examples.  Some analytical depth is lost through typographical errors and absence of rigorous statistical testing, but the analysis remains good, relevant and mostly supportedâcharacteristic of the top of the 4-6 band.

The essay follows a logical academic structure: abstract-style header, table of contents, sequential sections (background, methodology, results, analysis, conclusion) and extensive appendices.  Headings and sub-headings facilitate navigation, and the structure clearly supports understanding, meeting the 3-4 band description.

The argument flows from results to a balanced conclusion, acknowledging limitations (sample size, similar encode times, need for longer videos) and considering scenarios where each codec might be preferred.  Evaluation is present but somewhat cursoryâerror sources and alternative methodologies are not deeply explored.  This constitutes a good discussion with some critical evaluation, aligning with the mid-upper range (4-6) but not reaching the coherence and depth required for 7-9.

Specialist terms such as âentropy codingâ, âquadtreeâ, âmulti-symbol arithmetic encodingâ, âR-squaredâ and âBig-Oâ are used frequently and usually correctly.  Occasional slips (âDiscreateâ, âprecision is reduced which further reduces the file sizeâ without quantisation nuance) show that mastery is not flawless, yet the terminology remains mostly accurate and coherent, meriting the upper half of the 3-4 band.

The research (background theory, choice of metrics, HandBrake scripting, statistical treatment) is clearly relevant to answering the stated question, and the student integrates technical sources with practical experimentation.  While some referencing is perfunctory and the theoretical justification for linear vs quadratic time is limited, the alignment between gathered evidence and the question is consistent, fulfilling the descriptor for the top of the 4-6 band.

Consistent font, figure captions, tables and code blocks are used.  Graphs and images are embedded and referenced.  Word count, title and section breaks appear at the start, and (in the submitted document) page numbers are visible.  These layout choices fully support evaluation of the work, satisfying the upper band for presentation.

The student demonstrates good knowledge of video-coding concepts (CTU, PU, CABAC, CDEF, IDTX) and time-complexity theory, drawing from a range of academic sources.  Concepts are generally correct, though some explanations are surface-level or contain minor inaccuracies (e.g. mis-spelling âdiscreateâ and conflating processing time with Big-O without formal justification).  Application of theory to the experiment is decent but not consistently deep, placing the work solidly in the 3-4 band.

The research question is quoted verbatim at the start: âTo what extent is there a time complexity and output storage space difference between HEVC and AV1 codecs in terms of encoding?â.  It is narrow (two clearly measurable variables, two specific codecs) and it remains directly referenced throughout data collection, analysis and the conclusion, showing effective connection to the discussion.  While phrasing is slightly awkward, focus and integration meet the descriptor for the 5-6 band.

The student states the overall focusâcomparing time complexity and output file size of HEVC and AV1âclearly in the title, abstract-style opening and introduction.  The motivation (importance for streaming platforms) is explicitly linked to the two performance metrics that will be measured.  Headings, a contents page and repeated reminders of purpose keep the reader oriented, so the communication is accurate and effective.  Minor lapses in phrasing (e.g. âthese videos need smaller file sizeâ) do not obscure the focus, justifying a placement in the 5-6 band rather than lower.

annotations.comment: The research question is stated prominently and remains central throughout the report, demonstrating clear focus and connection to subsequent sections.
The inclusion of a word count at the start meets IB formatting requirements and reflects adherence to the maximum 4 000-word limit.
The candidate code is clearly displayed, but consider repositioning it under a standard header rather than inline to enhance presentation clarity.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well organized, supporting navigation through the investigationâs structure.
Consider adding dot leaders between section titles and page numbers to improve visual alignment and reader usability in the contents.
The introduction frames the streaming challenge effectively, but the sentence âvideo codecs with encode the dataâ is awkward. Revise for clarity (e.g., âcodecs encode raw video dataâ).
The motivation linking encoding time and file size to server resources and user experience shows clear understanding of practical relevance.
Terminology like âHEVCâ and âAV1â is introduced accurately, but including publication years in citations (e.g., Haivision, n.d.) would strengthen academic precision.
Your background section defines video codecs well, but expanding on how encoding parameters (bitrate, resolution) directly tie to complexity could deepen understanding.
The explanation of CTUs, CUs and quadtrees is technically clear; graphics (Figure 1) reinforce the description effectively.
In the prediction subsection, the last sentence is cut off. Ensure complete paragraphs to maintain coherence and reader comprehension.
The overview of transformation and quantization uses accurate terms (DCT, DST, TUs), though âDiscreate Sine Transformâ is a typoâcorrect to âDiscrete Sine Transformâ.
Your explanation of entropy coding and CABAC is comprehensive, demonstrating excellent grasp of context modeling and arithmetic coding processes.
Equations are correctly formatted in LaTeX; ensure all variables (e.g., $x$, $y$) are defined immediately to avoid reader confusion.
When introducing AV1âs multi-symbol encoding, including a brief comparison to HEVCâs single-symbol context enhances critical evaluation of algorithmic trade-offs.
Procedural steps are detailed and logical, though adding code snippets inline could immediately illustrate script structure without referring to appendices.
The time complexity section defines Big O effectively and uses an illustrative figure to support understanding of asymptotic trends.
Independent, dependent, and control variables are well identified, but justify selection of video sample criteria to strengthen methodological rigor.
The list of control variables covers key factors. Consider grouping related items under sub-headings to improve reader clarity.
Raw data tables are presented clearly, but including units in column headers (e.g., âTime (s)â) would improve immediate interpretability.
The data-processing section correctly derives frames per video, but explicitly linking this step to the research question would reinforce analytical relevance.
Graphs are used effectively to visualize encoding time and file-size trends, supporting comparative analysis across codecs.
When fitting models, you adjust the y-intercept to zero; justify this choice statistically to avoid introducing bias in intercept estimation.
The comparison of linear vs quadratic fits using RÂ² is sound; consider adding p-values or confidence intervals to strengthen statistical robustness.
The conclusion reflects on limitations and suggests extending the experiment, showing evaluative reflection on methodology.
Discussion recognizes qualityâcompression trade-offs but could further evaluate how CDEF and IDTX impact perceptual metrics, not just file size.
Bibliography entries are formatted consistently in APA style, demonstrating attention to source accuracy and consistency.

rubricData.name: Computer Science EE
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: The Computer Science Extended Essay is a 4,000-word independent research project that allows students to explore a specific topic within computer science. This essay emphasizes technical analysis and original thought, encouraging students to delve into areas such as algorithm efficiency, software development, or the impact of computing technologies. By engaging in the EE, students apply their theoretical knowledge to practical scenarios, enhancing their problem-solving and analytical skills, while contributing original insights to the field of computer science.
